nodes.label: The contest between local-first tooling and cloud sync…
nodes.text: The contest between local-first tooling and cloud sync is really a contest over ownership. Local-first keeps the source of truth on your machine, treats the network as an optimization rather than a dependency, and degrades gracefully when offline. Engrex leans hard into this: the daemon owns the SQLite store and the embeddings never leave the device unless you explicitly export them.

nodes.label: Cosine distance vs. cosine similarity for ranking…
nodes.text: Cosine distance is 1 minus cosine similarity. When we store edge weights we keep the distance (smaller = more similar) because most vector indexes return distances directly. For rendering, similarity = 1 - distance gives a clean 0..1 strength we can map to link opacity and width.

nodes.label: Force-directed layouts trade determinism for legibility…
nodes.text: Force-directed layouts trade determinism for legibility. Every run can settle differently, but clusters of related nodes fall together and outliers drift to the edge. The charge (many-body) force pushes nodes apart, links pull neighbors together, and a weak centering force keeps the whole thing on screen.

nodes.label: Embeddings are lossy compressions of meaning…
nodes.text: Embeddings are lossy compressions of meaning into a fixed-length vector. Two chunks that talk about the same idea in different words land near each other; a chunk that shares vocabulary but not meaning may sit surprisingly far away. This is why a knowledge graph over embeddings surfaces conceptual neighbors, not just keyword matches.

nodes.label: Chunking strategy shapes retrieval quality…
nodes.text: Chunking strategy shapes retrieval quality more than the embedding model does, most of the time. Too small and each chunk loses context; too large and a single vector has to represent several ideas at once, blurring its position in the space. Sentence-window and semantic chunking both try to keep one coherent idea per chunk.

nodes.label: SQLite as an application file format…
nodes.text: SQLite as an application file format is an underused pattern. Instead of inventing a bespoke on-disk layout, you get transactions, a query language, and battle-tested durability for free. Engrex stores chunks, embeddings, and relations in one file that the user can back up by copying it.

nodes.label: The second-brain promise and its failure modes…
nodes.text: The second-brain promise is that capture plus retrieval beats memory. The failure mode is a graveyard of notes you never revisit. A graph view fights this by making relationships visible: you rediscover an old note because it lit up next to something you wrote today.

nodes.label: Daemon architecture keeps the UI thin…
nodes.text: A daemon architecture keeps the UI thin. The long-lived process owns the store, the embedding pipeline, and the HTTP surface on localhost:7778. The web front-end and the native macOS app are both just clients that render whatever the daemon serves, which means they never disagree about the data.

nodes.label: Recency as a first-class signal in a note graph…
nodes.text: Recency deserves to be a first-class visual signal. Warm, bright nodes are things you touched recently; cool, dim nodes are older. Coloring by createdAt turns the graph into a rough timeline you can read at a glance, without a separate axis.

nodes.label: Offline-first means the CDN is the enemy…
nodes.text: Offline-first means the CDN is the enemy. Every remote asset is a way for the app to break on a plane or behind a firewall. Vendoring D3 into the web directory keeps the whole visualization working with the network unplugged, which is the entire point of a local-first tool.
